title: Foundations of Programming for Business Analytics
description: This course will introduce students to two of the most popular and widely used languages in analytics: Python and R. Various packages, libraries, and functions associated with these languages will be covered in detail. The course is intended to prepare students for advanced courses that use one or both of these languages, i.e., the intended audience comprises students who want to gain a solid understanding of programming in the context of analytics.
skills_taught: Python, R

title: Programming for Data Science
description:
In the era of big data, it is critical to utilize said data to extract useful knowledge and
insights. The primary objective of this course is to introduce students to various data
analytics techniques for extracting business intelligence. Along the way we will develop
familiarity with Python, a widely used programming language across academia and
industry. The course will cover both basic programming concepts in Python as well as
useful packages for data science, including NumPy, Pandas, Polars, Matplotlib, Seaborn
and Scikit-Learn.
Prerequisites: BUAN 6356, BUAN 6383, MIS 6386, MIS 6334, MIS 6356, MIS 6382.
skills_taught: Python, Programming Fundamentals, Data Manipulation, Feature Engineering, Anomaly Detection, Regression Analysis

title: Big Data
description:
Master the rapidly evolving Big Data landscape with this comprehensive course, delving into Spark
and the essential components of the Hadoop ecosystem. Not only will you gain a thorough
understanding of core Spark and Hadoop concepts, but you'll also learn to expertly wield industrystandard tools such as Sqoop, Hive, Impala, Pig, Flume, and Spark to efficiently develop end-to-end
Big Data applications. Perfect for both professionals and students, this course equips you with the
skills to tackle the complex challenges faced by today's enterprises and research institutions.
skills_taught: Big Data, Hadoop, HDFS, Impala, Hive, Data Modeling, Spark, Data Aggregation, Spark SQL

title: Advanced Big Data Analytics
description: The course covers Spark using Scala in a Hadoop environment. The topics include Scala syntax, Spark streaming, GraphX, MLlib, and other features of Spark. This advanced course requires students to have prior skills and working knowledge of big data environment and Python functional programming.
skills_taught: scala programming, spark programming, big data processing, spark streaming, graph analytics using graphx, machine learning with mllib, distributed data processing, hadoop ecosystem, functional programming concepts

title: AWS Cloud Analytics
description: This course integrates data engineering, machine learning, and AI services to build enterprise analytics platforms. Students will create data pipelines for ingesting, storing, processing, and visualizing batch and streaming data. The course covers selecting and applying machine learning models, including foundation models and LLMs, to solve business problems. Through hands-on labs, students will model, build, train, and deploy custom ML models and AI algorithms. Aligned with AWS Data Engineering and Machine Learning certifications, this course prepares students to lead data-driven AI initiatives
skills_taught: data engineering, data pipeline development, data ingestion, data storage architecture, batch data processing, streaming data processing, data visualization, machine learning model development, model training, model deployment, ai model development, foundation models, large language models, enterprise analytics platforms, cloud data engineering, aws, aws data engineering tools, aws machine learning services

title: Robotic Process Automation
description:
This course is intended to provide students with practical literacy on robotic process automation
through real-world, relevant data preparation use cases. It will help identify potential uses and the
benefits and considerations for robotic process automation. The students will learn the elements
of a business process and the basics of developing a BPM application, implementing triggers to
automate processes, defining and measuring KPIs. Students will use elements of artificial
intelligence (AI) and machine learning capabilities to handle high-volume, repeatable tasks that
previously required humans to perform. These tasks can include queries, calculations, and
maintenance of records and transactions. Students will be able to use apply analytics to the
generated data for a systematic computational analysis of data for the discovery, interpretation,
and communication of meaningful patterns in data that will be used towards effective decision
making
skills_taught: robotic process automation, business process management, business process modeling, process orchestration, process reengineering, process optimization, bpm application development, workflow automation, automation triggers, kpi development and measurement, data preparation, artificial intelligence applications, machine learning applications, task automation, query automation, transaction processing automation, data analytics, pattern discovery, data interpretation, decision making analytics

title: Business Data Warehousing
description: Provides in-depth knowledge of data warehousing principles, data warehouse techniques, and business intelligence systems. Discusses the topics of data warehouse design, Extract-Transform-Load (ETL), data cubes, and data marts. Will create business intelligence using data warehouses with several OLAP and analytical tools. SAP, Business Objects, Cognos, or other data warehousing tools will be used to illustrate data warehousing concepts.
skills_taught: data warehousing, data warehouse architecture, data warehouse design, extract transform load (etl), data integration, data cubes, data marts, business intelligence systems, online analytical processing (olap), multidimensional data analysis, business intelligence reporting, analytical tools, sap, sap businessobjects, ibm cognos

title: Cloud Computing Fundamentals
description:
This course is designed as a primer to discuss the information technology that is impacting
our businesses and changing our lives: cloud computing. The course is designed to
understand the fundamentals of cloud computing and then deep dive into Amazon Web
Services (AWS), Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and GenAI in Google Cloud, so the
students will build a T-Shape structure: Multi-cloud and Hybrid-cloud.
The course examines Cloud Computing from both technology and business perspectives,
so students will explore how Cloud Computing revolutionizes diverse business sectors. It
aims to deliver a holistic and balanced view of the business model, technology framework,
data intelligence, and security practice of Cloud Computing, and thus provide concepts
and ideas for the technology students to address business challenges and the business
students to decode technology encryptions. It serves as a launchpad to develop our
visionary technology and business leaders in the future.
skills_taught: cloud computing, multi cloud architecture, hybrid cloud architecture, cloud computing fundamentals, cloud service models, cloud infrastructure, cloud security, cloud business models, cloud data intelligence, amazon web services aws, google cloud platform gcp, generative ai in cloud, cloud based application development, cloud deployment strategies, cloud solutions for business

title: AWS Cloud Solution Architecture
description:
This course is designed as a primer for our
graduate students to learn the Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud computing and security. The

pillars: operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, and cost

certifications
skills_taught: amazon web services aws, cloud computing architecture, aws well architected framework, operational excellence in cloud, cloud security, cloud reliability, performance efficiency in cloud systems, cloud cost optimization, cloud architecture design, cloud infrastructure management, aws solution architecture, cloud deployment strategies

title: Robotics and Financial Technology
description: This course focuses on different robotic technologies used in finance.
skills_taught: robotic process automation, financial automation, robotics in finance, process automation tools, business process management, automation technologies, financial workflow automation, task automation in finance

title: Organizing for Business Analytics Platforms
description:
The course develops conceptual understanding of platforms for business analytics and key business drivers
that lead to business initiatives. The course examines how decision-makers in key functional areas of an
enterprise rely on data teams, how teams identify and develop analytical and engineering techniques to solve
business problems, and how data analytics platforms are adopted successfully. The course also emphasizes
the development of business cases for strategic analytics initiatives and discusses best practices for
descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics.
skills_taught: business analytics platforms, business drivers, data-driven decision making, analytical techniques, engineering techniques, solving business problems, data teams collaboration, strategic analytics initiatives, business case development, descriptive analytics, predictive analytics, prescriptive analytics, adoption of analytics platforms, enterprise analytics

title: Applied Machine Learning
description: This course covers machine learning models for business data including text mining, natural language processing, non-linear regression models, resampling methods and advanced neural networks and artificial intelligence-based models for data-driven analytics. The course will be taught using either R or Python language.
Prerequisites: (BUAN 6356 or BUAN 6383 or MIS 6386 or BUAN 6324 or MIS 6324 or OPRE 6399) and (OPRE 6359 or BUAN 6359).
skills_taught: machine learning, applied machine learning, business data analytics, text mining, natural language processing, non linear regression, resampling methods, neural networks, artificial intelligence models, data-driven analytics, r programming, python programming

title: Applied Natural Language Processing
description:
This is an advanced course focusing on natural language processing and the utility of textual data to gain
meaningful quantitative and actionable insights about the language (mainly English) using rule-based and
statistical methods and to extract the information for real-world applications. This class will focus both on
modern neural methods for these problems (including architectures such as RNNs, LSTMs,
Transformers) and on classical methods (logistic/linear regression).
skills_taught: natural language processing, text analytics, rule-based data analysis, statistical data analysis, machine learning for text, human language understanding, social media text analysis, business text analysis, unstructured data analysis, textual data interpretation, actionable insights extraction

title: Advanced Business Analytics with R
description:
This course covers theories and applications of business analytics. The focus is on
extracting business intelligence from internal or external business data for various
applications. Topics include data manipulation, imputation, variable selection, as well as
advanced analytic methods, including (but not limited to) business data analytics,
modeling, customer analytics, web intelligence analytics, business performance analytics,
and decision-making analytics. The emphasis is placed on the 'know-how' -- knowing
how and why to extract and apply business analytics to improve business decisionmaking. Students will also acquire hands-on experience with business analytics software,
specifically R
Prerequisites: (BUAN 6356 or MIS 6356 or OPRE 6305) and (OPRE 6359 or BUAN 6359)
skills_taught: business analytics, business intelligence, data manipulation, data imputation, variable selection, advanced analytic methods, business data analytics, predictive modeling, customer analytics, web intelligence analytics, business performance analytics, decision-making analytics, r programming, hands-on business analytics, data-driven decision making

title: Modeling for Business Analytics
description:
This is a fast-paced course that starts with an introduction of popular approaches in business analytics (e.g., preprocessing, dimensionality reduction, association rules, clustering, basics of classification), proceeds into advanced
methods (e.g., additional classification models, ensemble methods), and concludes with advanced models in customer
analytics (e.g., discrete time models, continuous time models, count models, choice models, zero-inflated models,
finite mixture models, models for non-contractual settings). While the tool of choice will be Python, the focus of the
course will be on modeling (i.e., this is not a course intended to teach you Python) – familiarity with Python is
assumed.
OnlyOneOfThese: (MIS 6334 or OPRE 6334 or BUAN 6357 or MIS 6357) and (BUAN 6383 or MIS 6386)
skills_taught: business analytics, data preprocessing, dimensionality reduction, association rules, clustering, classification models, ensemble methods, customer analytics, discrete time models, continuous time models, count models, choice models, zero-inflated models, finite mixture models, non-contractual customer models, predictive modeling, advanced modeling techniques, python programming

title: Applied Deep Learning
description: Covers fundamentals of Deep Learning applied to business data. Includes various neural network architectures such as Feedforward Artificial Neural Networks, Convolutional Neural Networks, and Transformer Models for Computer Vision in different business scenarios.
skills_taught: deep learning, applied deep learning, neural networks, feedforward neural networks, convolutional neural networks, transformer models, computer vision, business data analytics, predictive modeling, business applications of ai, deep learning architectures

title: Causal Analytics and A/B Testing
description:
All managers need to make decisions to achieve specific objectives. For example, a firm like Spotify wants to grow its customer base (it's objective). It intends to send promotional emails to
potential subscribers to achieve its goal. So, it must decide what design of promotional email messages would help it get the maximum number of new subscribers. This is a cause and effect
question because the design of the email message (cause) induces a person receiving the email to purchase the subscription (effect). Common sense dictates that Spotify can analyze email
designs used in the past to ascertain the most effective email design. Unfortunately, this common-sense approach is wrong! This is because email designs that are correlated with high
subscription rates may not be better. To identify the best email design, Spotify must make a distinction between correlation and causation in data. The gold standard in making this
distinction is a randomized control trial (aka, an A/B Test). Organizations and policymakers in government constantly grapple with such causal questions.
For example, firms want to know how does a price change affect the sales of a product, or how to create websites that increase consumer engagement and lead generation; political parties
want to know what messaging on social media sites can boost their political influence, and governments want to know whether allowing parents to pay for private schools using publicly
funded vouchers make the education system more effective. In particular, firms rooted in the digital economy like Netflix, Airbnb, eBay, Groupon,
Booking.com, Uber and Amazon, etc. make extensive and continuous use of A/B tests and have a dedicated team of data scientists and IT-personnel to implement, monitor and analyze such
tests. Banks and Insurance companies constitute another important sector where the use of A/B testing is ubiquitous.
While A/B tests are the best way to answer causal questions, sometimes they are too expensive, too time taking, or even unethical to implement. In such situations, the only recourse for data
scientists is to use observational data to tease out the causal effects of an intervention. Therefore, the course will focus on the design and analysis of A/B tests and also on statistical
techniques that can be used with observational data to achieve reliable causal inferences. We will employ a combination of lectures, cases, and other assignments to discuss the course
material. The approach is hands-on, exposing students to simulated and real-world datasets, and equipping them with the tools and experience they can leverage immediately on the job.
skills_taught: causal analytics, a/b testing, correlation vs causation, experimental design, statistical analysis, observational data analysis, causal inference, marketing analytics, promotional effectiveness analysis, data-driven decision making, practical data analysis, real-world dataset analysis

title: Data Visualization
description: Studies the technologies, techniques and algorithms for the creation of effective data visualization in the context of data science. Explores topics related to data wrangling, insight modeling, cognitive science, and graphical communication. Develops practical skills using data visualization tools including SAP Lumira, Tableau, Excel Powerview, and D3. Primary objective will be creation of data visualizations for strategic communication.
skills_taught: data visualization, data science visualization, data wrangling, insight modeling, cognitive science for visualization, graphical communication, strategic data communication, sap lumira, tableau, excel powerview, d3.js, data storytelling, visualization design techniques

title: The American Healthcare System
description:
This course is centered on understanding how the American healthcare system works from the ground up. It is designed for students who either plan to build a career in healthcare management or simply want to become more informed about the system they interact with as patients and citizens.
The course starts by tracing the historical development of healthcare in the United States, how the system evolved over time, what forces shaped it, and why it looks the way it does today. From there, it moves into the structural side of healthcare, examining the different types of settings where care is delivered, such as hospitals, outpatient facilities, and community health organizations, as well as the wide range of professionals who make up the healthcare workforce.

A significant portion of the course is dedicated to how healthcare is financed. Students will explore how money flows through the system, from insurance models and managed care to accountable care organizations and pay-for-performance structures, and how financial decisions affect the quality and accessibility of care. Students will also examine the relationship between cost and measurable outcomes, which is one of the central tensions in American healthcare policy.

The course also dives into health policy and regulation, looking at how laws get made, how public sector policymakers implement change, and how advocacy and organized efforts influence the direction of the system. Alongside this, students will engage with the major ethical, legal, and equity-related issues that providers, insurers, and policymakers navigate on a daily basis, including social drivers of health and population health management.

Finally, the course looks forward, covering emerging areas like health information technology, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence in clinical and administrative settings, and interoperability between health systems. Guest speakers from the industry are brought in throughout the semester to ground these topics in real, current practice.

The course is highly interactive, built around discussions, team-based case presentations, and written briefings, ensuring that students do not just learn the content but can also analyze, communicate, and apply it in a professional context.
skills_taught: Healthcare System Navigation, Health Policy Analysis, Financial Management in Healthcare, Regulatory & Legal Compliance, Ethical Decision-Making, Data Analysis & Outcome Measurement, Critical Thinking & Problem Solving, Medical Terminology,  Emerging Technology Evaluation (AI, Health IT, Cybersecurity), Research & Information Synthesis, Written Communication (Briefing Papers), Oral Presentation & Public Speaking, Team Collaboration & Project Management, Peer Evaluation & Feedback, Stakeholder Communication, Healthcare Workforce & Operations Knowledge, Population Health Management, Strategic Decision-Making

title: Healthcare Informatics
description:
This course sits at the intersection of healthcare and technology, exploring how digital information systems are designed, implemented, and used to transform raw clinical and administrative data into meaningful knowledge that drives better decisions. At its foundation, the course is about understanding how technology can make healthcare smarter — reducing medical errors, cutting unnecessary costs, and ultimately improving patient outcomes.
The course begins by establishing a clear picture of the healthcare IT landscape today — where it came from, how it has evolved, and what the major systems and architectures look like in practice. Students will learn how data flows through a healthcare organization, from the moment a patient walks in the door to the billing, reporting, and compliance processes that follow. This includes a deep look at Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) and Electronic Health Records (EHRs), which are the backbone of modern clinical information management, as well as the major legislation that has shaped how these systems are built and adopted across the industry.
A significant portion of the course focuses on the challenge of making different healthcare systems talk to each other — known as interoperability — and the data standards that make that possible. Students will examine how healthcare organizations build and manage their information architectures, how databases are structured and queried, and how data integration works across complex, multi-system environments.
The course also covers the business and strategic side of health IT, including how organizations evaluate, acquire, and govern technology investments, how to calculate the total cost of ownership of a system, and how IT planning aligns with broader organizational strategy. Students will work through real-world frameworks for making technology decisions in resource-constrained healthcare environments.
Analytics and artificial intelligence represent another major thread running through the course. Students will explore how big data and AI are being applied in clinical and administrative healthcare settings, what the real-world challenges of implementing these tools look like, and how to think critically about whether an AI solution is actually creating value. Emerging technologies including telehealth, cloud computing, and ubiquitous connectivity are also covered, giving students a forward-looking perspective on where the field is heading.
Throughout the course, students work with real case studies and practical assignments, ensuring that the concepts covered are grounded in actual healthcare industry problems rather than theory alone.
skills_taught: Health IT Systems Knowledge, Electronic Medical Records (EMR/EHR) Management, Healthcare Data Architecture & Integration, Database Management & SQL, Interoperability & Data Standards, Healthcare Analytics, Artificial Intelligence, Applications in Healthcare, IT Governance & Project Management, Strategic IT, Planning & Alignment, Health IT Legislation & Regulatory Compliance, Privacy & Cybersecurity in Healthcare, Technology Investment Evaluation, Critical Thinking & Evidence-Based Decision Making, Data Transformation & Information Architecture Design, Population Health & Reimbursement Reform Understanding, Case Analysis & Problem Solving, Team Collaboration, Written & Oral Communication

title: Healthcare Operations Management
description:
This course is about understanding and improving the processes that make healthcare organizations run efficiently. At its core, it asks a fundamental question — how do you take limited resources, whether that is staff, equipment, time, or supplies, and organize them in a way that delivers the best possible care to the most people, at the lowest cost, with the shortest wait? These are not abstract questions. They are the daily challenges faced by hospital administrators, clinic managers, and healthcare executives, and this course gives students the analytical toolkit to tackle them head-on.
The course begins with process flow analysis, which is the foundation of operations management. Students learn how to map out how patients, information, and resources move through a healthcare system, identify where bottlenecks occur, and calculate how those bottlenecks affect capacity, wait times, and overall efficiency. From there, the course moves into project management, teaching students how to plan and execute complex healthcare initiatives on time and within budget, accounting for uncertainty along the way.
Inventory management is another major topic, covering how healthcare organizations decide how much of a supply to keep on hand, when to reorder, and how to balance the cost of holding inventory against the risk of running out. Closely related is queueing theory, which gives students a mathematical framework for understanding why waiting lines form in healthcare settings and what can be done to reduce them — a directly applicable skill in emergency departments, clinics, and surgical scheduling.
The course then introduces linear programming, a powerful optimization technique that helps managers make the best possible decision when facing multiple competing constraints, such as budget limits, staffing caps, and patient demand. Students use Excel Solver to apply these methods to real healthcare scenarios. The course also covers healthcare analytics through regression modeling, quality control methods for monitoring and improving process performance, and supply chain management principles including how the healthcare supply chain behaves and how disruptions like the bullwhip effect can ripple through the system.
Throughout the semester, students apply these techniques to a real-world group project, conducting original analysis on an actual healthcare issue and presenting their findings to stakeholders — mirroring exactly what is expected of operations analysts and managers in the field.
skills_taught: Process Flow Analysis & Bottleneck Identification, Capacity Planning & Resource Optimization, Project Management & Scheduling, Inventory Management & Supply Chain Analysis, Queueing Theory & Wait Time Reduction, Linear Programming & Optimization, Healthcare Analytics & Regression Modeling, Quality Control & Performance Measurement, Excel & Solver Application, Data-Driven Decision Making, Operations Strategy & Process Improvement, Supply Chain Management, Outcome Measurement & Management, Critical Thinking & Quantitative Problem Solving, Team Collaboration & Group Project Management, Written & Oral Communication, Stakeholder Presentation & Reporting, Healthcare Policy & Operations Context

title: Electronic Health Records Applications
description:
This course is unlike most traditional graduate courses — it is built around doing, not just learning. Rather than studying electronic health records from a purely theoretical standpoint, students get direct, hands-on access to EPIC, the most widely used electronic medical record system in the United States, and work through real clinical and administrative scenarios inside the software itself. This makes the course one of the most practically valuable in the healthcare management program, because students graduate with direct experience in the exact tools they will encounter on the job.
The course begins by establishing a foundational understanding of what an EMR and EHR actually are, how they differ, and why they matter to every stakeholder in the healthcare system — from physicians and nurses to administrators, billing staff, and IT managers. From there, students dive into workflow analysis, learning how to map, evaluate, and improve the processes that govern how patients move through a healthcare system and how information flows alongside them. This is a critical skill because poorly designed workflows are one of the leading causes of inefficiency, errors, and frustration in clinical settings.
A large portion of the course is dedicated to exploring the major functional areas of EPIC, guided by staff from UT Southwestern Medical Center. Students work hands-on through patient registration and scheduling, ambulatory and outpatient care workflows, inpatient care workflows for both providers and nurses, revenue cycle management and billing, specialty and ancillary modules, and clinical decision support and reporting tools. Each of these areas represents a core operational domain in any major healthcare organization, and understanding how they connect within a single integrated system is what separates effective healthcare IT professionals from those who only understand one piece of the puzzle.
The course also addresses the broader strategic and regulatory landscape surrounding EHR systems. Students examine the regulatory and market-driven pressures that shape how EHRs are adopted and used, the challenges of integrating EHR systems with other enterprise platforms, and what it takes to implement, upgrade, and sustain a large-scale clinical information system over time. The course closes with a forward-looking discussion of emerging trends — including accountable care organizations, telemedicine, and the future direction of health IT — giving students a complete picture of where the field is heading.
skills_taught: EPIC EMR Software Proficiency, Electronic Health Record (EHR) Operations & Workflow, Clinical Workflow Analysis & Mapping, Patient Registration & Scheduling Systems, Ambulatory & Inpatient Care Workflow Management, Revenue Cycle & Billing Operations, Clinical Decision Support & Reporting, Data & System Integration, EHR Implementation & Lifecycle Management, Healthcare IT Architecture & Interoperability, Regulatory & Compliance Knowledge in Health IT, Telemedicine & Emerging Technology Awareness, Critical Thinking & Organizational Problem Solving, Hands-On Lab-Based Technical Skills, Written & Oral Communication, Class Participation & Professional Engagement

title: Healthcare Analytics
description:
This course is designed for students who want to understand how data and analytics can be used to solve some of the most pressing problems in healthcare — rising costs, inefficient resource allocation, uneven care quality, and missed opportunities to use existing data more effectively. It sits at the intersection of healthcare management and data science, giving students both the conceptual framework to think analytically about healthcare problems and the hands-on technical skills to actually build and implement analytics solutions.
The course begins by introducing the broader landscape of analytics — what it is, how it is applied across industries, and why healthcare in particular has so much untapped potential for data-driven improvement. From there, students are introduced to R and RStudio, the primary tools used throughout the course, learning how to navigate the software, retrieve and prepare data, and perform foundational analytical functions. No prior programming experience is assumed, and the course builds these skills progressively from the ground up.
A significant portion of the course is dedicated to statistical and predictive modeling techniques. Students learn exploratory data analysis, simple and multiple regression, classification methods including decision trees and logistic regression, and clustering techniques such as hierarchical and k-means clustering. All of these methods are taught in the context of real healthcare problems, so students are always connecting the technical work back to meaningful business and clinical questions — such as predicting patient outcomes, segmenting populations for targeted interventions, or identifying inefficiencies in care delivery.
Data visualization and communication is another key focus. Students learn how to present analytical findings in ways that are clear, compelling, and actionable for decision makers — a skill that is just as important as the analysis itself in professional settings. The course covers effective dashboard design and data storytelling, ensuring students can bridge the gap between complex analysis and practical organizational decision making.
The course culminates in a group project where students select a real healthcare analytics problem, source and analyze data, and present their findings to the class — simulating exactly the kind of work expected of analytics professionals in hospitals, insurance companies, consulting firms, and health IT organizations.
Prerequisites: OPRE 6301 or SYSM 6303 or BUAN 6359 or OPRE 6359.
skills_taught: Healthcare Data Analytics, R & RStudio Programming, Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), Simple & Multiple Regression Analysis, Classification Modeling & Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, Clustering & Segmentation Techniques, Machine Learning Foundations, Data Visualization & Dashboard Design, Data Storytelling & Communication, Data Retrieval & Preparation, Predictive Modeling, Business Intelligence in Healthcare, Critical Thinking & Evidence-Based Decision Making, Healthcare Delivery & Policy Context, Project Design & Management, Team Collaboration & Peer Evaluation, Written & Oral Presentation Skills

title: Organizing for Business Analytics Platforms
description:
This course is built around one of the most important and often overlooked questions in modern business analytics — it is not just about knowing how to analyze data, but understanding how organizations build, manage, and scale the platforms and teams that make analytics possible in the first place. While many analytics courses focus on the technical side of modeling and statistics, this course takes a broader, more strategic view, examining how enterprises actually organize themselves around data and what it takes to turn raw data into a sustained competitive advantage.
The course begins by establishing why data transformation has become a critical priority for modern organizations. Students explore the full data lifecycle — how data is generated, ingested, stored, processed, and ultimately used to drive decisions — and examine the distinct but interconnected roles of data analytics, data engineering, data operations, data science, and artificial intelligence within an enterprise. This gives students a holistic vocabulary and mental model for understanding how all the pieces of a modern data organization fit together.
From there, the course dives into the technical architecture side of analytics platforms. Students explore modern big data architectures including data lakes, lakehouses, and cloud-native platforms, with a particular focus on Microsoft Azure and Snowflake as leading industry tools. Through hands-on labs, students get direct experience working with these platforms — ingesting data, managing storage layers, optimizing performance, and understanding how cloud infrastructure supports large-scale analytics. The course also covers data quality, data lineage, data wrangling, and real-time streaming data, ensuring students understand not just how to build analytics solutions but how to keep them reliable and trustworthy over time.
A significant thread running through the course is the business case for analytics. Students examine how decision-makers in key functional areas rely on data teams, how to identify and frame analytics opportunities, and how to develop and present strategic business cases for data initiatives. This is reinforced through a series of Harvard Business Review case studies drawn from real companies — including Airbnb and others — where students debate and analyze how organizations have navigated complex data challenges and turned them into business opportunities.
The course also covers machine learning and generative AI foundations, exploring why high-quality data is the prerequisite for any successful ML project, and introducing core concepts in natural language processing and computer vision. Data governance, privacy, and security in the age of AI round out the curriculum, ensuring students understand the ethical and regulatory responsibilities that come with managing data at scale.
skills_taught: Cloud Data Platform Knowledge (Azure & Snowflake), Data Architecture & Modern Big Data Patterns, Data Ingestion & Pipeline Management, Data Storage & Retrieval Optimization, Data Quality & Lineage Management, Data Wrangling & Transformation, Real-Time & Streaming Data Processing, Machine Learning Foundations & AI Concepts, Natural Language Processing & Computer Vision Awareness, Data Governance, Privacy & Security, Business Case Development for Analytics Initiatives, Strategic Analytics Planning & Platform Adoption, Case Study Analysis & Business Problem Solving, Data Engineering & DataOps Understanding, Cloud Computing Fundamentals, Critical Thinking & Decision Making, Team Collaboration & Group Project Management, Written & Oral Presentation Skills

title: Causal Analytics and A/B Testing
description:
This course tackles one of the most fundamental and consequential challenges in data-driven decision making — the difference between correlation and causation. It is a distinction that sounds simple but is routinely misunderstood, and getting it wrong leads organizations to make confident decisions based on misleading data. This course gives students the theoretical grounding and practical tools to get it right.
The course opens by building a deep appreciation for why observational data alone is almost never sufficient to answer causal questions. When a company sees that customers who received a certain email design subscribed at higher rates, does that mean the email design caused the increase? Not necessarily — and this course explains precisely why not, and what to do instead. The answer, in most cases, is a well-designed experiment, and the gold standard for that is the randomized controlled trial, more commonly known in business settings as an A/B test.
Students learn the full lifecycle of A/B testing — from understanding the statistical foundations of hypothesis testing and confidence intervals, to designing experiments that are properly powered and free from common pitfalls, to analyzing results using regression analysis and selecting the right outcome metrics. The course also addresses more advanced design challenges such as interference between test and control groups, intention-to-treat effects, and issues of compliance, all of which are real problems that arise when running experiments in messy, real-world environments.
A major strength of the course is its honest acknowledgment that A/B tests are not always possible. They can be too costly, too slow, or ethically off-limits. For these situations, the course introduces a powerful set of observational methods for causal inference — including fixed effects models, difference-in-difference analysis, propensity score matching, natural experiments, and quasi-experiments. These are the tools that data scientists at major firms use when they cannot run a controlled experiment but still need to draw reliable causal conclusions from data.
The course is deeply hands-on. Students use R and RStudio throughout, working with real and simulated datasets to apply everything they learn. A live simulation tournament toward the end of the semester puts students in the role of decision makers running an actual experimental platform, making real-time choices and competing against their peers — making it one of the most experiential components of any analytics course in the program.
skills_taught: A/B Test Design & Implementation, Causal Inference & Causal Reasoning, Hypothesis Testing & Statistical Significance, Confidence Interval Estimation, Regression Analysis for Causal Questions, Power Analysis & Sample Size Determination, Difference-in-Difference Analysis, Fixed Effects Modeling, Propensity Score Matching, Quasi-Experimental Methods & Natural Experiments, Observational Data Analysis, Outcome Metric Selection & Evaluation, R & RStudio for Statistical Analysis, Experimental Platform Management, Business Decision Making with Data, Critical Thinking & Analytical Problem Solving, Team Collaboration & Group Assignment Management, Written & Oral Communication

title: Marketing Management
description:
This course provides graduate students with a comprehensive foundation in marketing — how it works, why it matters, and how to apply it strategically in a competitive, global business environment. It is not a course about memorizing marketing definitions. It is designed to develop the kind of analytical, strategic thinking that marketing professionals and business leaders use when making real decisions about products, customers, pricing, distribution, and communication.
The course is built heavily around the case method, which means students spend a significant amount of time stepping into the shoes of real managers at real companies — analyzing the situation they faced, evaluating the options available to them, and defending a clear, data-supported recommendation. Cases covered in the course span a wide range of marketing contexts, from Dell's early e-commerce strategy and Starbucks' customer service investment decisions, to new product launches, digital direct-to-consumer marketing at Hubble Contact Lenses, AI-powered e-commerce at THE YES, and pharmaceutical pricing strategy at The Medicines Company. Each case forces students to apply marketing frameworks to a unique, ambiguous business problem — exactly the kind of challenge they will face in their careers.
The course covers all the core pillars of marketing strategy and management. Students learn how to analyze the marketing environment, understand consumer behavior, and apply segmentation, targeting, and positioning frameworks to identify and reach the right customers. Product strategy and new product development are explored in depth, including how to move an innovation from concept to market successfully. The course also covers marketing research methods, channel and distribution strategy, promotional planning across traditional and digital media, and pricing strategy — including the ethical dimensions of pricing decisions.
A major component of the course is a group marketing project where students develop a full marketing plan for an original product or service idea that does not yet exist commercially. This involves defining the problem the product solves, analyzing the competitive landscape, estimating market size, designing the marketing mix, and presenting the plan to the class as if pitching to investors. This project ties together everything covered in the course and gives students a tangible, end-to-end experience in strategic marketing planning.
skills_taught: Marketing Strategy & Planning, Consumer Behavior Analysis, Market Segmentation, Targeting & Positioning, New Product Development & Innovation, Marketing Research & Demand Estimation, Pricing Strategy & Ethics, Promotional Planning & Media Strategy, Distribution & Channel Management, Digital & Direct-to-Consumer Marketing, Case Analysis & Business Problem Solving, Data-Driven Decision Making, Competitive Analysis & SWOT, Marketing Plan Development, Quantitative Analysis & Financial Estimation, Oral Presentation & Persuasive Communication, Written Business Communication, Team Collaboration & Peer Evaluation, Critical Thinking & Analytical Reasoning

title: Marketing Data Analysis and Research
description:
This course sits at the intersection of marketing and data science, teaching students the rigorous methods and analytical techniques that underpin sound marketing decisions. While marketing is often associated with creativity and intuition, this course makes clear that the most effective marketing is grounded in systematic research, carefully collected data, and statistically defensible analysis. Students leave the course equipped to design, execute, and interpret marketing research studies from start to finish.
The course begins by establishing the foundation of marketing research — how to define a research problem clearly, develop a research approach, and choose the right research design for the question at hand. Students explore the full landscape of data sources available to marketers, including secondary data and qualitative methods such as focus groups and in-depth interviews, before moving into the design of primary research instruments. A significant portion of the course is dedicated to survey design — how to write effective questions, choose the right measurement and scaling approaches, and construct a questionnaire that produces reliable, actionable data.
From there, the course moves into the statistical side of marketing research. Students learn sampling techniques and how to determine appropriate sample sizes, followed by a thorough grounding in the analytical methods used to make sense of survey data. This includes frequency distributions, cross-tabulations, hypothesis testing, ANOVA and ANCOVA, correlation analysis, and regression modeling. These are not abstract statistical exercises — every technique is taught in the context of real marketing decisions, so students always understand not just how to run an analysis but what the results mean for a business.
The course also dedicates meaningful attention to data visualization and storytelling — how to take complex analytical findings and present them in a way that is clear, compelling, and persuasive to clients and senior management. This is a critical professional skill that many analytically strong students overlook, and the course treats it as an essential part of the research process rather than an afterthought.
The capstone of the course is a two-part group research project in which students design and conduct an actual marketing research study. Teams define a research problem, develop a survey instrument, collect at least fifty real responses, analyze the data using the statistical methods covered in the course, and produce a full written research report complete with executive summary, findings, and recommendations. This end-to-end project mirrors exactly what marketing research professionals do in practice and gives students a polished, real-world deliverable for their portfolio.
Prerequisites: MKT 6301 and (OPRE 6301 or BUAN 6359 or OPRE 6359 or MKT 6379)
skills_taught: Marketing Research Design & Methodology, Survey Design & Questionnaire Development, Primary & Secondary Data Collection, Measurement & Scaling Techniques, Sampling Methods & Sample Size Determination, Descriptive Statistics & Frequency Analysis, Cross-Tabulation & Hypothesis Testing, ANOVA & ANCOVA Analysis, Correlation & Regression Analysis, Data Visualization & Dashboard Storytelling, Research Report Writing & Executive Communication, Consumer Behavior Analysis, Market Size Estimation & Forecasting, Qualitative Research Methods, Causal & Descriptive Research Design, Critical Thinking & Analytical Reasoning, Team Collaboration & Peer Evaluation, Written & Oral Presentation Skills

title: Pricing Analytics
description:
This course is built around one of the most consequential decisions any business makes — how to price its products and services. As one Harvard Business School professor famously put it, pricing is the moment of truth where all of marketing strategy comes into focus. Get it right and a company can dramatically improve profitability without changing a single thing about its product. Get it wrong and even the best product in the market can fail. This course gives students the analytical framework and strategic thinking to get it right.
The course is organized around what it calls the three C's of pricing — Costs, Competition, and Customers — and uses this structure to systematically work through every dimension of a sound pricing decision. From the customer side, students learn how to estimate the economic value a product delivers to buyers, how price sensitivity works and what drives it, how psychological factors and perceptions of fairness shape buying behavior, and how to identify and exploit meaningful differences across customer segments. From the competitive side, students examine how to price strategically in markets with rivals, including how to avoid destructive price wars, when cooperative pricing makes sense, how price leadership works, and how differentiation affects pricing power. From the company side, students learn how to think about relevant costs, credible commitment, and long-term profitability rather than short-term revenue maximization.
The course then builds on this foundation to cover a wide range of pricing strategies and structures that are relevant in today's business environment. These include value-in-use analysis, price discrimination across customer segments, bundling strategies, product-line pricing, dynamic pricing over the product lifecycle, channel pricing, and the use of AI and machine learning in modern pricing systems. Psychological pricing and the ethics and sustainability of pricing decisions are also addressed, ensuring students develop not just technical competence but sound judgment about when and how pricing strategies should be applied.
A distinctive feature of the course is its use of real business case studies drawn from diverse industries and contexts — from Toblerone's airport duty-free pricing strategy and Tata Nano's failed low-cost car launch in India, to Altius Golf's fighter brand dilemma, JCPenney's disastrous "Fair and Square" pricing experiment, Uber's regulatory battles in Hong Kong, and a complex water rights pricing scenario in the American West. Each case forces students to apply the frameworks they have learned to a messy, real-world situation and defend a clear recommendation — exactly the kind of work expected of pricing analysts, strategy consultants, and marketing managers in practice.
skills_taught: Strategic Pricing Framework & Decision Making, Economic Value Analysis & Value Communication, Customer Segmentation for Pricing, Price Sensitivity & Demand Analysis, Psychological Pricing & Behavioral Economics, Price Discrimination & Bundling Strategies, Product-Line & Channel Pricing, Dynamic Pricing & Lifecycle Pricing, Competitive Pricing Strategy & Price War Analysis, Incremental Break-Even & Profitability Analysis, AI & Machine Learning Applications in Pricing, Pricing Ethics & Sustainability Considerations, Case Analysis & Strategic Recommendation, Excel-Based Pricing Modeling & Quantitative Analysis, Critical Thinking & Analytical Reasoning, Written Business Communication, Oral Presentation & Defense of Recommendations, Team Collaboration & Peer Evaluation

title: Social Media Marketing and Insights
description: This course is designed to provide students with a theoretical foundation and working knowledge of social media strategies and tools used by marketing departments and agencies. Students are exposed to best practices in social media marketing for brand awareness improvement, lead generation, customer relationship management, viral campaign, influencer marketing, and Generative AI-assisted content creation. The course also provides a strategic foundation for social media analytics in both B-C and B-B environments, including AI-powered social listening and media analysis, platform selection, attribution and ROI measurement of social media interventions using various metrics. The course includes a hands-on component.

title: MarTech Ecosystem
description:
This course is designed for students who want to understand how modern marketing actually gets executed at scale — through technology. In today's business environment, marketing is no longer just about creativity and strategy. It is powered by a complex and interconnected set of tools, platforms, and data systems collectively known as the Marketing Technology stack, or MarTech. This course gives students a comprehensive, end-to-end understanding of how that ecosystem works and how to build, manage, and optimize it to deliver measurable business results.
The course begins by establishing what a customer-centric data ecosystem looks like and why it matters. Students learn how customer data is collected, integrated, and activated across multiple marketing touchpoints — from email and social media to websites, e-commerce platforms, and beyond. A central focus is on Customer Data Platforms, which serve as the backbone of modern personalized marketing, and how they connect with CRM systems, marketing automation tools, content management systems, and analytics platforms to create a seamless, unified view of the customer.
Personalization is a major theme throughout the course. Students explore how advanced marketing technologies enable brands to deliver the right message to the right person at the right time — at scale. This includes dynamic content creation, automated customer journey mapping, and predictive segmentation, all of which are increasingly powered by artificial intelligence. Generative AI is given particular attention as a transformative force in the MarTech landscape, enabling marketers to automate content generation, personalize interactions in real time, and make smarter decisions about where and how to deploy marketing spend.
The course also addresses the strategic and operational challenges of building an effective MarTech stack. Students learn how to evaluate and select external marketing technology vendors and partners, how to integrate third-party solutions into an existing ecosystem, and how to ensure that the technology choices an organization makes are aligned with its broader marketing and business goals. SEO, SEM, social media tools, testing and optimization platforms, and e-commerce integrations are all covered, giving students exposure to the full breadth of tools that marketing teams use in practice.
Performance measurement is woven throughout the course. Students learn how to define and track the right KPIs for different marketing objectives — from brand awareness and customer engagement to upsell, retention, and return on marketing investment. The course closes with a look at marketing privacy regulations and the future trends shaping the MarTech landscape, ensuring students are prepared not just for today's environment but for where the field is heading.
skills_taught: MarTech Stack Design & Optimization, Customer Data Platform (CDP) Management, Marketing Automation & CRM Integration, Personalization Strategy at Scale, Generative AI for Content Creation & Marketing, Predictive Analytics & Customer Journey Mapping, Multi-Channel Marketing Strategy & Orchestration, SEO & SEM Tools & Strategy, Social Media Marketing Tools, Content Management Systems (CMS), E-commerce & MarTech Integration, Data Integration & Customer Data Management, Marketing Performance Measurement & KPI Tracking, ROI & Attribution Modeling, AI-Enhanced Marketing Decision Making, Testing & Optimization Techniques, Vendor Evaluation & Partner Management, Marketing Privacy & Compliance Awareness, Critical Thinking & Strategic Planning, Team Collaboration & Group Project Management

title: Marketing Web Analytics and Insights
description:
This course is designed for students who want to understand how web analytics drives modern digital marketing decision-making. In today's business environment, marketing performance is no longer guesswork,  it is measured, tracked, and optimized through data. This course gives students a comprehensive understanding of how to collect, analyze, and act on web data to deliver measurable marketing results.

The course begins by establishing the foundational concepts of web analytics, including key metrics, KPIs, and digital marketing principles. Students learn how data is captured and reported across platforms, with a central focus on Adobe Analytics, covering everything from basic reporting to advanced techniques like segment building, conversion funnels, tag management, and data visualization.

Actionable insight is a major theme throughout the course. Students explore how raw analytics data can be transformed into clear, evidence-based business recommendations. This includes hands-on experience with real transactional datasets, report building, and dashboard creation tailored to stakeholder needs.

The course also integrates Generative AI as a transformative force in marketing analytics. Students learn how AI tools can automate report generation, optimize multichannel content, build predictive customer segmentation models, and personalize user experiences at scale.

The second half of the course shifts focus to Google Analytics, covering it in depth across multiple weeks through lectures, a group project, and hands-on application. The course closes with students sitting for the official Google Analytics Certification, ensuring they leave with not just practical knowledge but a recognized industry credential.
skills_taught: Web Analytics Fundamentals & Strategy, Adobe Analytics Configuration & Reporting, Metrics & KPI Definition, Digital Marketing Performance Evaluation, Visitor Behavior Analysis & Reporting, Data Visualization & Dashboard Creation, Segment Builder & Customer Segmentation, Conversion Funnel Setup & Optimization, Tag Management & Debugging, Report Builder & Workspace Analysis, Excel-Based Analytics, Google Analytics Implementation & Reporting, A/B Testing & Optimization, Generative AI for Marketing Analytics, Automated Report Generation, Predictive Analytics & Customer Segmentation, Multichannel Content Optimization, Data-Driven Business Recommendations, Marketing Performance Measurement, Team Collaboration & Group Project Management

title: Customer Analytics and Insights
description:
This course equips students with the analytical techniques and critical thinking skills needed to harness marketing data for real business impact. At its core, the course focuses on understanding customer behavior through data, teaching students how to analyze, interpret, and activate marketing datasets to identify high-value prospects, acquire new customers, and retain the most profitable ones over time.

A central emphasis is placed on retail and omnichannel data, reflecting the reality that today's customers interact with brands across multiple touchpoints, in-store, online, mobile, and beyond. Students learn how to work with data across these channels to build a complete picture of the customer and make smarter marketing decisions.

Key analytical techniques covered include Life-Time Value modeling, which helps marketers understand the long-term revenue potential of individual customers; RFM analysis (Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value), a powerful segmentation framework for prioritizing marketing efforts; response analysis, which measures how customers react to marketing campaigns; and attribution modeling, which identifies which touchpoints and channels deserve credit for driving conversions.

Beyond the technical methods, the course places strong emphasis on developing critical thinking and problem-solving skills. Students are challenged not just to run analyses, but to interpret what the data means, draw meaningful conclusions, and translate those findings into clear, actionable recommendations for management. The ability to tell a compelling story with data, and present it persuasively to decision-makers — is treated as just as important as the analysis itself.
Prerequisites: OPRE 6301 or BUAN 6359 or OPRE 6359 or MKT 6379.
skills_taught: Customer Data Analysis & Interpretation, Life-Time Value (LTV) Modeling, RFM Analysis & Customer Segmentation, Response Analysis & Campaign Measurement, Attribution Modeling & Channel Credit Analysis, Retail & Omnichannel Data Management, Customer Prospecting & Acquisition Strategy, Customer Retention & Profitability Analysis, Data-Driven Decision Making, Marketing Performance Measurement & KPI Tracking, Multichannel Customer Journey Analysis, Predictive Analytics & Behavioral Modeling, Data Visualization & Insight Presentation, Actionable Insight Development & Storytelling, Critical Thinking & Problem Solving, Strategic Marketing Recommendations, Management Communication & Presentation Skills, Team Collaboration & Group Project Management

title: Advanced Marketing Web Analytics and Insights
description:
This course is designed for students who want to push beyond the fundamentals of web analytics and develop true mastery of advanced techniques across the full spectrum of modern digital data sources. In today's connected world, marketing data no longer comes from a single channel — it flows from websites, mobile apps, marketing platforms, and an ever-expanding network of IoT devices. This course teaches students how to harness all of that data and turn it into strategic, high-impact decisions.
The course begins by deepening students' understanding of web analytics across multiple platform types — desktop, mobile, and app environments — giving them the skills to work with complex, multi-source datasets that reflect how real consumers interact with brands today. From there, students learn how to connect web analytics data to basic AI models, using those insights to optimize eCommerce platform performance and sharpen digital marketing strategy in meaningful, measurable ways.
A major focus of the course is data visualization and real-time reporting. Students learn how to integrate web analytics data with Tableau, one of the industry's leading visualization tools, to build dynamic, real-time dashboards that track conversion funnels, marketing channel performance, and customer segments. These dashboards are designed not just for analysis, but for communication — giving stakeholders a clear, live view of marketing performance.
The course also provides a thorough grounding in A/B testing, covering the full lifecycle from test design and measurement planning to data collection, analysis, and insight generation. Students leave with the ability to design and execute rigorous experiments that drive continuous improvement across digital marketing initiatives.

title: Intelligent Enterprise Systems with SAP
description: This course provides students with an understanding of intelligent enterprise systems using the SAP S/4 HANA platform. The course focuses on managing enterprise-wide business processes such as Design to Operate, Record to Report (Core Finance), Lead to Cash, Source to Pay, and Hire to Retire, and integrations among them in large organizations. title: Supply Chain Software with SAP
description: This course introduces supply chain planning with software such as SAP's S/4 HANA and Integrated Business Planning (IBP) with case discussions and lab exercises. Topics include fundamentals of ERP and SAP, master and transaction data, MRP, forecasting, supply and demand matching. This course is intended for students with interests in software-based supply chain management or digital supply chains. Emerging trends including Artificial Intelligence and Blockchain Solutions in supply chain systems will also be discussed. No prior SAP experience required.

title: Applied Cybersecurity Analytics and Risk Management
description: Students will explore IT Security and Analytics, perform hands-on exercises identifying security gaps with simulated data (application logs, network monitor logs, firewall logs, etc), and create predictions about potential security threats that could exploit the gaps. This course allows students to get an in-depth exposure to cybersecurity concepts and topics including security and risk management (legal, regulatory compliance), asset security (data classification, ownership, data security, and privacy), security engineering (security architecture, design, and security models), telecommunication and network security (perimeter protection, network attacks, IDS, IPS, firewalls), identity and access management (authentication, authorization, identity as a service), security assessment and testing, security operations (business continuity, disaster recovery, incident management, vulnerability and patch management), cryptography, and software development security. They will evaluate simulated data to identify security flaws and predict an organization's security position.

description: This course prepares business decision makers to recognize the threats and vulnerabilities present in current information systems and how to design and develop secure systems. title: Digital Forensics and Incident Management
description: This course discusses methods and techniques for responding to security incidents and breaches and in-depth coverage of digital forensics of client devices, databases, web servers, application servers, and computer networks. title: Information Technology Audit and Risk Management
description: Management's role in designing and controlling information technology used to process data is studied. title: Preparing for Cybersecurity Threats
description: Threats from cyber criminals always exist, but the level of preparation and investment in cybersecurity varies greatly between organizations. This course discusses the current threat environment and specific risk mitigation countermeasures that should be deployed. title: Financial Accounting

title: Financial Management
description: Covers fundamental corporate finance concepts and their application to managing company finances. Topics include financial planning, capital budgeting, investment evaluation, and financial performance analysis.

description: Introduces blockchain technology and its applications across modern business environments including digital payments, ownership verification, smart contracts, and supply chain finance. title: Operations Management
description: Provides an overview of operations management and the processes required to deliver products and services effectively. title: Analytics for Innovation in Demand and Supply Management
description: Examines operational and pricing challenges in demand and supply management including supply chain configuration, supplier contracts, inventory management, product allocation, and dynamic pricing. title: Spreadsheet Modeling and Analytics
description: Focuses on building analytical models using spreadsheets for business decision making. Students learn effective spreadsheet design, optimization techniques, simulation modeling, sensitivity analysis, and decision analysis to evaluate trade-offs, constraints, and uncertainty across business functions such as finance, marketing, and operations.
skills_taught: spreadsheet modeling; optimization modeling; simulation analysis; sensitivity analysis; decision modeling; quantitative business analysis

title: Risk and Decision Analysis
description: Provides an overview of analytical methods used in risk assessment, risk management, and decision analysis. skills_taught: risk analysis; decision analysis; probabilistic modeling; risk management strategies; reliability analysis; decision optimization

title: Demand and Revenue Analytics
description: Examines analytical approaches used to manage demand and revenue in industries with capacity constraints and perishable products such as airlines, hotels, and retail. Students learn forecasting methods that incorporate pricing and economic factors and explore revenue management strategies including overbooking, dynamic pricing, and demand forecasting.
